steps.prompt: A goalkeeper's view of the goal
steps.subtitle: Picture the goal posts as two fixed points, A and B, on a circle. A [central angle](teal) has its corner at the center O; an [inscribed angle](red) has its corner ON the circle, at a point C. Tap each one to begin.

graphic.name: goal-angle
graphic.caption: From anywhere on the circle, the goal opens up the same angle in a player's view. The center sees a wider angle than the players on the rim.

targets.label: Central angle
targets.hint: The central angle's corner is at the center, O.

targets.label: Inscribed angle
targets.hint: The inscribed angle's corner is on the circle, at C.

steps.doneNote: Both found — the central angle's corner is at O, the inscribed angle's corner is on the circle at C. Both are built from the same two points A and B; this lesson finds how they're linked.

steps.subtitle: Keep A and B fixed and slide C along the circle. Watch closely: does the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB change, or stay the same?

steps.prompt: Now meet O — compare the two angles
steps.subtitle: The center O makes the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB. Drag any point and compare it with the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB. Which is bigger, and by how much?

steps.prompt: What did you notice? True or false?
steps.subtitle: You compared the [inscribed angle](red) with the [central angle](teal) on the same arc. Judge each claim.

cards.statement: An inscribed angle is half the central angle that opens onto the same arc.
cards.note: Exactly — that's the inscribed angle theorem. The corner on the circle sees half of what the center sees.

cards.statement: The central angle is the smaller of the two.
cards.note: The other way round — the central angle (at O) is the bigger one; the inscribed angle is half its size.

cards.statement: As C slides around its arc, the inscribed angle ∠ACB keeps changing size.
cards.note: It stays the same. Every point on that arc sees A and B at the same angle — the goalkeeper's view.

cards.statement: When A and B are the ends of a diameter, the inscribed angle ∠ACB is 90°.
cards.note: Yes — the central angle is a straight 180°, and half of 180° is 90°. This is Thales' theorem.

cards.statement: If the central angle ∠AOB is 120°, then ∠ACB is 240°.
cards.note: Halve, don't double: 120° ÷ 2 = 60°. The inscribed angle is always the smaller one.

steps.doneNote: Inscribed is half the central angle, it holds anywhere on the arc, and on a diameter it's always 90°.

steps.body:
An [inscribed angle](red) is always HALF the [central angle](teal) that opens onto the same arc.
When ∠AOB is 120°, ∠ACB is 60°. Flip it around: the central angle is double the inscribed angle.
Remember the corners: the inscribed angle's corner sits ON the circle, the central angle's corner sits at the center O.

blanks.prompt: OC splits the 60° inscribed angle ∠ACB into two equal base angles. Each is 60 ÷ 2 = ?
Isosceles triangle OAC has base angles of 30° each, so ∠AOC = 180 − 2 × 30 = ?
∠AOB = 120° and the inscribed ∠ACB = 60°. The central angle is 120 ÷ 60 = ? times the inscribed angle.

stages: Draw a radius from O out to A, B, and C — every radius of a circle is the same length.
Two equal radii make triangles OAC and OBC isosceles, and an isosceles triangle has equal base angles.
OC splits the 60° inscribed angle, so those base angles at A, B, and C are each 30°.
A triangle's angles add to 180°, so ∠AOC and ∠BOC are each 120°. The angles at O fill a full turn of 360°, leaving ∠AOB = 120°.
120° = 2 × 60° — the inscribed angle is half the central angle.

steps.intro: Use the theorem — [inscribed](red) is half the [central angle](teal) — one step at a time.

parts.prompt: The central angle ∠AOB is 120°. Halve it to find the inscribed angle ∠ACB: 120 ÷ 2 = ?
Now A and B are the ends of a diameter, so ∠AOB = 180°. What is the inscribed angle ∠ACB?

options.feedback: That's the central angle — the inscribed angle is half of it.
Halve the central angle, don't double it.

parts.note: Half of 180° is 90° — a right angle. That's Thales' theorem.

parts.prompt: Reverse it: an inscribed angle ∠ACB is 70°. The central angle is double, so ∠AOB = 70 × 2 = ?
parts.note: ∠AOB = 140°. Going from inscribed to central, you double.

steps.doneNote: Half to go from central to inscribed, double to go back — and on a diameter the inscribed angle is always 90°.

feedback.incorrect: The inscribed angle is half the central angle.
The central angle is double the inscribed angle.
The inscribed angle is half the central angle.
